title: Rapunzel
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 12, S. 65–69. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly, including daß, Thurm/Thüre, gieng/anfieng/fieng, ward, erschrack, gerieth, däuchte, Scheere, Noth and Thränen.
about: The twelfth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 12), the classic story of the maiden in the doorless tower. A childless couple’s craving for rampion (Rapunzel) from a sorceress’s walled garden costs them their daughter; shut in the tower, the girl lets down her long hair for the witch — and later for a king’s son — until the secret slips out, and after blindness and long wandering the lovers are reunited. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, Thurm, gieng, ward and Thränen.

sentences.t: Es war einmal ein Mann und eine Frau, die wünschten sich schon lange vergeblich ein Kind, endlich machte sich die Frau Hoffnung der liebe Gott werde ihren Wunsch erfüllen.
sentences.l: There was once a man and a woman, who had long wished in vain for a child; at last the woman made herself the hope that the dear God would grant her wish.
sentences.i: Once upon a time there was a man and a woman who had long wished in vain for a child; at last the woman began to hope that the dear Lord would grant her wish.
sentences.n: “Es war einmal” is the fixed fairy-tale opening, ‘once upon a time.’ “werde ... erfüllen” is subjunctive I of reported hope (she hopes God *would* grant it) — this reported-speech subjunctive turns up constantly in Grimm.

sentences.t: Die Leute hatten in ihrem Hinterhaus ein kleines Fenster, daraus konnte man in einen prächtigen Garten sehen, der voll der schönsten Blumen und Kräuter stand; er war aber von einer hohen Mauer umgeben, und niemand wagte hinein zu gehen, weil er einer Zauberin gehörte, die große Macht hatte und von aller Welt gefürchtet ward.
sentences.l: The people had in their back-house a little window, out of which one could see into a magnificent garden that stood full of the most beautiful flowers and herbs; it was, however, surrounded by a high wall, and no one dared to go in, because it belonged to a sorceress who had great power and was feared by all the world.
sentences.i: At the back of their house these folk had a little window, from which one could look out into a splendid garden full of the finest flowers and herbs; but it was surrounded by a high wall, and no one dared go in, for it belonged to a sorceress who had great power and was feared by everyone.
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (‘became / was’); modern German uses wurde. You will meet ward throughout. “gehörte einer Zauberin” — gehören takes the dative: it belonged *to* a sorceress.

sentences.t: Eines Tags stand die Frau an diesem Fenster und sah in den Garten hinab, da erblickte sie ein Beet, das mit den schönsten Rapunzeln bepflanzt war: und sie sahen so frisch und grün aus, daß sie lüstern ward und das größte Verlangen empfand von den Rapunzeln zu essen.
sentences.l: One day the woman stood at this window and looked down into the garden; there she caught sight of a bed that was planted with the most beautiful rampion: and it looked so fresh and green that she grew filled with craving and felt the greatest longing to eat of the rampion.
sentences.i: One day the woman stood at this window and looked down into the garden, and she spied a bed planted with the finest rampion; and it looked so fresh and green that a craving seized her, and she felt the greatest longing to eat some of the rampion.
sentences.n: “daß” is the 1857 spelling of dass (‘that’); the ß is kept throughout this edition. “sahen ... aus” is the separable verb aussehen (‘to look, appear’) — the prefix aus- jumps to the end of the clause; German separable verbs do this constantly.

sentences.t: Das Verlangen nahm jeden Tag zu, und da sie wußte daß sie keine davon bekommen konnte, so fiel sie ganz ab, sah blaß und elend aus.
sentences.l: The longing increased every day, and since she knew that she could get none of it, she fell quite away, looked pale and wretched.
sentences.i: The longing grew stronger every day, and since she knew she could get none of it, she wasted quite away and looked pale and wretched.
sentences.n: Two more separable verbs: “nahm ... zu” (zunehmen, to increase) and “fiel ... ab” (abfallen, here to waste away). “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste.

sentences.t: Da erschrack der Mann und fragte „was fehlt dir, liebe Frau?“
sentences.l: Then the man took fright and asked, “what is lacking to you, dear wife?”
sentences.i: At that the man was alarmed and asked, “What is the matter, dear wife?”
sentences.n: “erschrack” is the 1857/archaic preterite of intransitive erschrecken, ‘took fright’; modern spelling is erschrak. “was fehlt dir?” (literally ‘what is lacking to you?’) uses the dative dir — the idiomatic ‘what’s wrong with you?’

sentences.t: „Ach,“ antwortete sie, „wenn ich keine Rapunzeln aus dem Garten hinter unserm Hause zu essen kriege, so sterbe ich.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” she answered, “if I get no rampion from the garden behind our house to eat, then I die.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” she answered, “if I cannot get some of the rampion from the garden behind our house to eat, I shall die.”
sentences.n: “kriege” is colloquial for bekomme (‘I get’). Note the “wenn ... so” frame: ‘if ... then.’ “unserm Hause” has the old dative -e ending.

sentences.t: Der Mann, der sie lieb hatte, dachte „eh du deine Frau sterben lässest, holst du ihr von den Rapunzeln, es mag kosten was es will.“
sentences.l: The man, who loved her, thought, “ere you let your wife die, you fetch her some of the rampion, it may cost what it will.”
sentences.i: The man, who loved her dearly, thought, “Before you let your wife die, you had better fetch her some of the rampion, cost what it may.”
sentences.n: “eh” is a colloquial/archaic form of ehe (‘before’). “lieb haben” = to love, hold dear. “es mag kosten was es will” is a set phrase, ‘cost what it may.’

sentences.t: In der Abenddämmerung stieg er also über die Mauer in den Garten der Zauberin, stach in aller Eile eine Hand voll Rapunzeln und brachte sie seiner Frau.
sentences.l: In the evening twilight he therefore climbed over the wall into the sorceress’s garden, cut in all haste a handful of rampion, and brought it to his wife.
sentences.i: So at dusk he climbed over the wall into the sorceress’s garden, hastily cut a handful of rampion, and brought it to his wife.
sentences.n: “stach” is the preterite of stechen, here of cutting/digging the plants quickly. “seiner Frau” is dative — brought it *to* his wife.

sentences.t: Sie machte sich sogleich Salat daraus und aß sie in voller Begierde auf.
sentences.l: She made herself salad from it at once and ate it up in full greed.
sentences.i: She at once made a salad of it and devoured it greedily.
sentences.n: “aß ... auf” is the separable verb aufessen (‘to eat up’). “sie” here refers back to the Rapunzeln (plural).

sentences.t: Sie hatten ihr aber so gut, so gut geschmeckt, daß sie den andern Tag noch dreimal so viel Lust bekam.
sentences.l: But they had tasted so good, so good to her, that the next day she got three times as much desire.
sentences.i: But it had tasted so very good to her that the next day she craved three times as much.
sentences.n: “ihr ... geschmeckt” — schmecken takes the dative person: it tasted good *to her*. “den andern Tag” = the next day (andern is the old spelling of anderen).

sentences.t: Sollte sie Ruhe haben, so mußte der Mann noch einmal in den Garten steigen.
sentences.l: Were she to have peace, then the man had to climb into the garden once more.
sentences.i: If she was to have any peace, the man had to climb into the garden once more.
sentences.n: “Sollte sie ...” — a conditional formed by putting the verb first (inversion), ‘if she was to ...’ “mußte” is the 1857 spelling of musste.

sentences.t: Er machte sich also in der Abenddämmerung wieder hinab, als er aber die Mauer herabgeklettert war, erschrack er gewaltig, denn er sah die Zauberin vor sich stehen.
sentences.l: So he betook himself down again in the evening twilight; but when he had climbed down the wall, he took fright mightily, for he saw the sorceress standing before him.
sentences.i: So at dusk he made his way down again; but when he had climbed down the wall, he was terribly frightened, for he saw the sorceress standing before him.
sentences.n: “sich hinab machen” = to make one’s way down. “herabgeklettert” (herabklettern, to climb down) is a separable verb; in the past participle the prefix herab- sits in front.

sentences.t: „Wie kannst du es wagen,“ sprach sie mit zornigem Blick, „in meinen Garten zu steigen und wie ein Dieb mir meine Rapunzeln zu stehlen? das soll dir schlecht bekommen.“
sentences.l: “How can you dare it,” she spoke with an angry look, “to climb into my garden and, like a thief, to steal me my rampion? that shall turn out ill for you.”
sentences.i: “How dare you,” she said with an angry look, “climb into my garden and steal my rampion like a thief? You shall pay dearly for it.”
sentences.n: “schlecht bekommen” is an idiom, ‘to turn out badly for someone’ (bekommen + dative). Note the dative “mir” in “mir meine Rapunzeln” — a dative of interest, ‘steal my rampion on me.’

sentences.t: „Ach,“ antwortete er, „laßt Gnade für Recht ergehen, ich habe mich nur aus Noth dazu entschlossen: meine Frau hat eure Rapunzeln aus dem Fenster erblickt, und empfindet ein so großes Gelüsten, daß sie sterben würde, wenn sie nicht davon zu essen bekäme.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” he answered, “let mercy go for justice; I resolved on it only out of need: my wife caught sight of your rampion from the window and feels so great a craving that she would die if she got none of it to eat.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” he answered, “temper justice with mercy! I brought myself to it only out of dire need: my wife caught sight of your rampion from the window, and she feels so great a craving that she would die if she could not get some of it to eat.”
sentences.n: “laßt Gnade für Recht ergehen” is a set idiom, ‘let mercy prevail over strict justice.’ “Noth” is the 1857 spelling of Not (‘need, distress’); the silent -th- recurs in this edition (Thurm, Thüre). “würde ... bekäme” are subjunctive II — the hypothetical ‘she would die if she got.’

sentences.t: Da ließ die Zauberin in ihrem Zorne nach und sprach zu ihm „verhält es sich so, wie du sagst, so will ich dir gestatten Rapunzeln mitzunehmen so viel du willst, allein ich mache eine Bedingung: du mußt mir das Kind geben, das deine Frau zur Welt bringen wird.
sentences.l: Then the sorceress let off in her wrath and spoke to him, “if it stands so, as you say, then I will allow you to take rampion along, as much as you will; only I make one condition: you must give me the child that your wife will bring into the world.
sentences.i: Then the sorceress’s anger relented, and she said to him, “If it is as you say, I will allow you to take away as much rampion as you like; only I make one condition: you must give me the child your wife will bring into the world.
sentences.n: “ließ ... nach” is the separable verb nachlassen (‘to let up, relent’). “verhält es sich so” — the reflexive sich verhalten means ‘to be the case.’ “zur Welt bringen” = to give birth to. “allein” here has the old sense ‘only, but.’

sentences.t: Es soll ihm gut gehen, und ich will für es sorgen wie eine Mutter.“
sentences.l: It shall go well for it, and I will care for it like a mother.”
sentences.i: It shall want for nothing, and I will care for it like a mother.”
sentences.n: “es” and “ihm” refer to das Kind (neuter), so grammatically ‘it,’ though the child is meant. “es soll ihm gut gehen” = ‘may it fare well’ (jemandem geht es gut, an impersonal dative idiom).

sentences.t: Der Mann sagte in der Angst alles zu, und als die Frau in Wochen kam, so erschien sogleich die Zauberin, gab dem Kinde den Namen Rapunzel und nahm es mit sich fort.
sentences.l: The man in his fear agreed to everything, and when the woman came into (child-)weeks, at once the sorceress appeared, gave the child the name Rapunzel, and took it away with her.
sentences.i: In his fear the man agreed to everything, and when the woman went into labour, the sorceress appeared at once, gave the child the name Rapunzel, and carried it off with her.
sentences.n: “sagte ... zu” is the separable verb zusagen (‘to agree, consent’). “in Wochen kommen” is an old idiom for going into childbed / giving birth (the Wochen are the weeks of confinement). “nahm ... mit ... fort” combines mitnehmen with fort, ‘took away with her.’

sentences.t: Rapunzel ward das schönste Kind unter der Sonne.
sentences.l: Rapunzel became the most beautiful child under the sun.
sentences.n: “ward” again — the archaic preterite of werden. “unter der Sonne” = ‘under the sun,’ i.e. anywhere on earth.

sentences.t: Als es zwölf Jahre alt war, schloß es die Zauberin in einen Thurm, der in einem Walde lag, und weder Treppe noch Thüre hatte, nur ganz oben war ein kleines Fensterchen.
sentences.l: When it was twelve years old, the sorceress shut it in a tower that lay in a wood and had neither stairs nor door; only quite at the top was a little window.
sentences.i: When she was twelve years old, the sorceress shut her in a tower that stood in a forest and had neither stair nor door; only right at the top was a little window.
sentences.n: “es” refers to Rapunzel as das Kind (neuter), hence grammatically ‘it’ — read it as ‘she.’ “schloß” is the 1857 spelling of schloss (past of schließen). “Thurm” and “Thüre” carry the old -th- (modern Turm, Tür). “weder ... noch” = ‘neither ... nor.’

sentences.t: Wenn die Zauberin hinein wollte, so stellte sie sich unten hin, und rief
sentences.l: When the sorceress wanted in, she placed herself down below, and called
sentences.i: When the sorceress wanted to get in, she would place herself down below and call:
sentences.n: “hinein wollte” — ‘wanted [to go] in’; the verb of motion is dropped after the modal. “stellte sich ... hin” is the separable reflexive sich hinstellen, ‘to plant oneself.’

sentences.t:
„Rapunzel, Rapunzel,
laß mir dein Haar herunter.“
sentences.l:
“Rapunzel, Rapunzel,
let your hair down to me.”
sentences.i:
“Rapunzel, Rapunzel,
let down your hair.”
sentences.n: “laß” is the archaic spelling of lass (imperative of lassen). Note the dative “mir” (‘to me’) in this first occurrence of the refrain — the later repetitions drop it, saying simply “laß dein Haar herunter.” “herunter” = down, toward the speaker below.

sentences.t: Rapunzel hatte lange prächtige Haare, fein wie gesponnen Gold.
sentences.l: Rapunzel had long, splendid hair, fine as spun gold.
sentences.n: “gesponnen” is the past participle of spinnen (‘to spin’); “wie gesponnen Gold” = like spun gold (the uninflected gesponnen is an older poetic usage).

sentences.t: Wenn sie nun die Stimme der Zauberin vernahm, so band sie ihre Zöpfe los, wickelte sie oben um einen Fensterhaken, und dann fielen die Haare zwanzig Ellen tief herunter, und die Zauberin stieg daran hinauf.
sentences.l: When she now heard the sorceress’s voice, she untied her braids, wound them at the top around a window-hook, and then the hair fell down twenty ells deep, and the sorceress climbed up on it.
sentences.i: Now whenever she heard the sorceress’s voice, she would untie her braids, wind them at the top around a window-hook, and then the hair would fall down twenty ells, and the sorceress would climb up by it.
sentences.n: “band ... los” is the separable verb losbinden (‘to untie’). “vernahm” (vernehmen) is an elevated word for ‘heard, perceived.’ An Elle (ell) is an old unit of length, roughly a forearm.

sentences.t: Nach ein paar Jahren trug es sich zu, daß der Sohn des Königs durch den Wald ritt und an dem Thurm vorüber kam.
sentences.l: After a couple of years it came about that the son of the king rode through the wood and came past the tower.
sentences.i: After a few years it came to pass that the king’s son was riding through the forest and passed by the tower.
sentences.n: “trug es sich zu” is the separable verb sich zutragen (‘to come about, happen’) — a solemn fairy-tale phrase. “kam ... vorüber” (vorüberkommen, to come past) is likewise separable.

sentences.t: Da hörte er einen Gesang, der war so lieblich, daß er still hielt und horchte.
sentences.l: There he heard a song that was so lovely that he stopped still and hearkened.
sentences.i: Then he heard a song so lovely that he halted and listened.
sentences.n: “hielt ... still” / “still hielt” — stillhalten, to hold still, stop. “horchte” (horchen) is ‘to listen intently, hearken.’

sentences.t: Das war Rapunzel, die in ihrer Einsamkeit sich die Zeit damit vertrieb, ihre süße Stimme erschallen zu lassen.
sentences.l: That was Rapunzel, who in her solitude passed the time therewith, to let her sweet voice ring out.
sentences.i: That was Rapunzel, who in her loneliness whiled away the time by letting her sweet voice ring out.
sentences.n: “sich die Zeit vertreiben” is an idiom, ‘to pass / while away the time.’ “erschallen lassen” = to let (a sound) ring out.

sentences.t: Der Königssohn wollte zu ihr hinauf steigen und suchte nach einer Thüre des Thurms, aber es war keine zu finden.
sentences.l: The king’s son wanted to climb up to her and searched for a door of the tower, but there was none to find.
sentences.i: The prince wanted to climb up to her and searched for a door into the tower, but none was to be found.
sentences.n: “hinauf steigen” (hinaufsteigen) — to climb up, away from the speaker; hinauf marks motion up and away. “des Thurms” is genitive (‘of the tower’).

sentences.t: Er ritt heim, doch der Gesang hatte ihm so sehr das Herz gerührt, daß er jeden Tag hinaus in den Wald gieng und zuhörte.
sentences.l: He rode home, yet the song had so greatly moved his heart that every day he went out into the wood and listened.
sentences.i: He rode home, but the song had touched his heart so deeply that every day he went out into the forest and listened.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (‘went’); this -ie- spelling of gehen/anfangen recurs in the edition (gieng, anfieng, fieng). “das Herz gerührt” — jemandem das Herz rühren, to move someone’s heart. “hörte ... zu” (zuhören) is separable.

sentences.t: Als er einmal so hinter einem Baum stand, sah er daß eine Zauberin heran kam und hörte wie sie hinauf rief
sentences.l: When he once stood thus behind a tree, he saw that a sorceress came near and heard how she called up
sentences.i: Once, as he stood thus behind a tree, he saw a sorceress come up, and heard her call up:
sentences.n: “kam ... heran” (herankommen, to come near, approach) and “rief ... hinauf” (hinaufrufen, to call up) are both separable.

sentences.t:
„Rapunzel, Rapunzel,
laß dein Haar herunter.“
sentences.l:
“Rapunzel, Rapunzel,
let your hair down.”
sentences.n: The refrain again — this time without the dative “mir” of the first occurrence.

sentences.t: Da ließ Rapunzel die Haarflechten herab, und die Zauberin stieg zu ihr hinauf.
sentences.l: Then Rapunzel let the hair-braids down, and the sorceress climbed up to her.
sentences.i: Then Rapunzel let down her braids of hair, and the sorceress climbed up to her.
sentences.n: “ließ ... herab” (herablassen, to let down) is separable.

sentences.t: „Ist das die Leiter, auf welcher man hinauf kommt, so will ich auch einmal mein Glück versuchen.“
sentences.l: “Is that the ladder on which one comes up, then I too will once try my luck.”
sentences.i: “If that is the ladder one climbs up by, then I too will try my luck for once.”
sentences.n: “Ist das ... , so ...” — a conditional made by putting the verb first (‘if that is ..., then ...’). “sein Glück versuchen” = to try one’s luck. (The prince is speaking to himself.)

sentences.t: Und den folgenden Tag, als es anfieng dunkel zu werden, gieng er zu dem Thurme und rief
sentences.l: And the following day, when it began to grow dark, he went to the tower and called
sentences.i: And the next day, when it began to grow dark, he went to the tower and called:
sentences.n: “anfieng” is the 1857 spelling of anfing (anfangen, ‘to begin’); it is separable (fing an), here inside a subordinate clause so it stays together. “Thurme” has the old dative -e.

sentences.t:
„Rapunzel, Rapunzel,
laß dein Haar herunter.“
sentences.l:
“Rapunzel, Rapunzel,
let your hair down.”

sentences.t: Alsbald fielen die Haare herab und der Königssohn stieg hinauf.
sentences.l: At once the hair fell down and the king’s son climbed up.
sentences.i: At once the hair fell down, and the prince climbed up.
sentences.n: “alsbald” is an elevated/archaic word for ‘at once, presently.’

sentences.t: Anfangs erschrack Rapunzel gewaltig als ein Mann zu ihr herein kam, wie ihre Augen noch nie einen erblickt hatten, doch der Königssohn fing an ganz freundlich mit ihr zu reden und erzählte ihr daß von ihrem Gesang sein Herz so sehr sei bewegt worden, daß es ihm keine Ruhe gelassen, und er sie selbst habe sehen müssen.
sentences.l: At first Rapunzel took fright mightily when a man came in to her, such as her eyes had never yet beheld; yet the king’s son began quite kindly to talk with her and told her that by her song his heart had been so greatly moved that it had left him no peace, and he had had to see her himself.
sentences.i: At first Rapunzel was terribly frightened when a man came in to her, the like of whom her eyes had never beheld; yet the prince began to speak to her very kindly, and told her that his heart had been so deeply moved by her singing that it had given him no peace, and he had had to see her for himself.
sentences.n: “fing an” (anfangen) shows the separable prefix split off to the end. In the reported speech the verbs stand in subjunctive I: “sei bewegt worden ... habe ... gelassen ... habe ... sehen müssen” — the subjunctive of reported speech, everywhere in Grimm. “einen” = ‘a man / one’ (masc. acc.), the object of erblickt hatten.

sentences.t: Da verlor Rapunzel ihre Angst, und als er sie fragte ob sie ihn zum Manne nehmen wollte, und sie sah daß er jung und schön war, so dachte sie „der wird mich lieber haben als die alte Frau Gothel,“ und sagte ja und legte ihre Hand in seine Hand.
sentences.l: Then Rapunzel lost her fear, and when he asked her whether she would take him for a husband, and she saw that he was young and handsome, she thought, “he will love me more than old Mother Gothel,” and said yes and laid her hand in his hand.
sentences.i: Then Rapunzel lost her fear, and when he asked her whether she would take him as her husband, and she saw that he was young and handsome, she thought, “He will love me better than old Mother Gothel,” and said yes, and laid her hand in his.
sentences.n: “zum Manne nehmen” = to take as one’s husband (Manne has the old dative -e). “lieber haben” = to prefer, love more (comparative of lieb haben). “Frau Gothel”: Gothel is a dialect word for ‘godmother’ — the sorceress’s name for herself.

sentences.t: Sie sprach „ich will gerne mit dir gehen, aber ich weiß nicht wie ich herab kommen kann.
sentences.l: She spoke, “I will gladly go with you, but I do not know how I can come down.
sentences.i: She said, “I will gladly go with you, but I do not know how I can get down.
sentences.n: “will gerne” — here wollen keeps its full sense ‘want to,’ reinforced by gerne (‘gladly’). “herab kommen” (herabkommen) is separable.

sentences.t: Wenn du kommst, so bring jedesmal einen Strang Seide mit, daraus will ich eine Leiter flechten und wenn die fertig ist, so steige ich herunter und du nimmst mich auf dein Pferd.“
sentences.l: When you come, then bring each time a skein of silk along; out of it I will plait a ladder, and when it is finished, then I climb down and you take me onto your horse.”
sentences.i: Whenever you come, bring a skein of silk each time, and out of it I will plait a ladder; and when it is finished, I will climb down, and you will take me up onto your horse.”
sentences.n: “bring ... mit” (mitbringen, to bring along) is separable — the imperative bring stays up front and mit goes to the end. “flechten” = to plait, braid, here of weaving a rope-ladder.

sentences.t: Sie verabredeten daß er bis dahin alle Abend zu ihr kommen sollte, denn bei Tag kam die Alte.
sentences.l: They arranged that until then he should come to her every evening, for by day the old woman came.
sentences.i: They agreed that until then he should come to her every evening, for by day the old woman came.
sentences.n: “bis dahin” = until then. “die Alte” = the old woman (an adjective used as a noun).

sentences.t: Die Zauberin merkte auch nichts davon, bis einmal Rapunzel anfieng und zu ihr sagte „sag sie mir doch, Frau Gothel, wie kommt es nur, sie wird mir viel schwerer heraufzuziehen, als der junge Königssohn, der ist in einem Augenblick bei mir.“
sentences.l: The sorceress noticed nothing of it either, until one day Rapunzel began and said to her, “do tell me, Mother Gothel, how does it come that you are much harder for me to pull up than the young king’s son — he is with me in a moment.”
sentences.i: And the sorceress noticed nothing of it, until one day Rapunzel began, and said to her, “Do tell me, Mother Gothel, how is it that you are so much harder for me to pull up than the young prince — he is with me in a moment?”
sentences.n: “sag sie mir” — the old polite third-person address (sie = ‘you,’ formal), so ‘tell me.’ Rapunzel innocently uses it to Gothel. “doch” is a softening particle (‘do ...’). “heraufzuziehen” (heraufziehen, to pull up) is separable, here as a zu-infinitive.

sentences.t: „Ach du gottloses Kind,“ rief die Zauberin, „was muß ich von dir hören, ich dachte ich hätte dich von aller Welt geschieden, und du hast mich doch betrogen!“
sentences.l: “Oh you godless child,” cried the sorceress, “what must I hear from you, I thought I had cut you off from all the world, and yet you have deceived me!”
sentences.i: “Oh, you wicked child!” cried the sorceress. “What must I hear from you! I thought I had cut you off from all the world, and yet you have deceived me!”
sentences.n: “hätte ... geschieden” — subjunctive II (‘I thought I *had* separated you’) inside reported thought. “von aller Welt scheiden” = to cut off from all the world. “betrogen” is the past participle of betrügen (‘to deceive’).

sentences.t: In ihrem Zorne packte sie die schönen Haare der Rapunzel, schlug sie ein paar mal um ihre linke Hand, griff eine Scheere mit der rechten, und ritsch, ratsch, waren sie abgeschnitten, und die schönen Flechten lagen auf der Erde.
sentences.l: In her wrath she seized Rapunzel’s beautiful hair, wound it a couple of times around her left hand, gripped a pair of scissors with the right, and snip, snap, they were cut off, and the beautiful braids lay on the ground.
sentences.i: In her fury she seized Rapunzel’s lovely hair, wound it a few times around her left hand, took a pair of scissors in her right, and snip, snap, it was cut off, and the beautiful braids lay on the ground.
sentences.n: “Scheere” is the 1857 spelling of Schere (‘scissors’). “ritsch, ratsch” is onomatopoeia for snipping (‘snip, snap’). “abgeschnitten” is the past participle of abschneiden (separable ab-).

sentences.t: Und sie war so unbarmherzig daß sie die arme Rapunzel in eine Wüstenei brachte, wo sie in großem Jammer und Elend leben mußte.
sentences.l: And she was so merciless that she brought the poor Rapunzel into a wilderness, where she had to live in great misery and wretchedness.
sentences.i: And she was so merciless that she carried poor Rapunzel off into a wilderness, where she had to live in great misery and want.
sentences.n: “Wüstenei” is an archaic/elevated word for a desolate waste or wilderness.

sentences.t: Denselben Tag aber, wo sie Rapunzel verstoßen hatte, machte Abends die Zauberin die abgeschnittenen Flechten oben am Fensterhaken fest, und als der Königssohn kam und rief
sentences.l: But that same day, on which she had cast Rapunzel out, in the evening the sorceress made the cut-off braids fast up at the window-hook, and when the king’s son came and called
sentences.i: But that very day, when she had cast Rapunzel out, the sorceress fastened the cut-off braids up on the window-hook that evening, and when the prince came and called:
sentences.n: “machte ... fest” (festmachen, to fasten) is separable. “verstoßen” = to cast out, disown. “am” = an dem.

sentences.t:
„Rapunzel, Rapunzel,
laß dein Haar herunter,“
sentences.l:
“Rapunzel, Rapunzel,
let your hair down,”

sentences.t: so ließ sie die Haare hinab.
sentences.l: so she let the hair down.
sentences.i: she let the hair down.
sentences.n: “ließ ... hinab” (hinablassen, to let down, away from the speaker) is separable. The “so” picks up from the call in the verse just before.

sentences.t: Der Königssohn stieg hinauf, aber er fand oben nicht seine liebste Rapunzel, sondern die Zauberin, die ihn mit bösen und giftigen Blicken ansah.
sentences.l: The king’s son climbed up, but above he found not his dearest Rapunzel, but the sorceress, who looked at him with evil and venomous glances.
sentences.i: The prince climbed up, but at the top he found not his beloved Rapunzel but the sorceress, who regarded him with evil, venomous looks.
sentences.n: “nicht ... sondern” = ‘not ... but (rather).’ “sah ... an” (ansehen, to look at) is separable.

sentences.t: „Aha,“ rief sie höhnisch, „du willst die Frau Liebste holen, aber der schöne Vogel sitzt nicht mehr im Nest und singt nicht mehr, die Katze hat ihn geholt und wird dir auch noch die Augen auskratzen.
sentences.l: “Aha,” she cried scornfully, “you want to fetch the lady-love, but the beautiful bird sits no longer in the nest and sings no more; the cat has fetched it and will yet scratch your eyes out too.
sentences.i: “Aha!” she cried scornfully, “you want to fetch your lady-love, but the pretty bird sits in the nest no more and sings no more; the cat has got it, and will scratch out your eyes as well.
sentences.n: “Frau Liebste” — ‘the lady-love, sweetheart.’ “nicht mehr” = no longer, no more. “die Augen auskratzen” (auskratzen, to scratch out) is separable.

sentences.t: Für dich ist Rapunzel verloren, du wirst sie nie wieder erblicken.“
sentences.l: For you Rapunzel is lost, you will never behold her again.”
sentences.i: For you Rapunzel is lost; you will never set eyes on her again.”
sentences.n: “verloren” is the past participle of verlieren (‘to lose’).

sentences.t: Der Königssohn gerieth außer sich vor Schmerz, und in der Verzweiflung sprang er den Thurm herab: das Leben brachte er davon, aber die Dornen, in die er fiel, zerstachen ihm die Augen.
sentences.l: The king’s son got beside himself with grief, and in his despair he sprang down from the tower: his life he carried off, but the thorns into which he fell pricked out his eyes.
sentences.i: The prince was beside himself with grief, and in his despair he leapt down from the tower: he escaped with his life, but the thorns into which he fell pricked out his eyes.
sentences.n: “gerieth” is the 1857 spelling of geriet (geraten, ‘to fall into a state’); “außer sich geraten” = to become beside oneself. “das Leben davonbringen” = to escape with one’s life. “zerstachen” (zerstechen) = pierced through — the prefix zer- means to pieces / apart.

sentences.t: Da irrte er blind im Walde umher, aß nichts als Wurzeln und Beeren, und that nichts als jammern und weinen über den Verlust seiner liebsten Frau.
sentences.l: There he strayed about blind in the wood, ate nothing but roots and berries, and did nothing but lament and weep over the loss of his dearest wife.
sentences.i: And so he wandered about blind in the forest, ate nothing but roots and berries, and did nothing but lament and weep over the loss of his beloved wife.
sentences.n: “irrte ... umher” (umherirren) is separable. “that” is the 1857 spelling of tat (thun = modern tun, ‘to do’). “nichts als” = nothing but.

sentences.t: So wanderte er einige Jahre im Elend umher und gerieth endlich in die Wüstenei, wo Rapunzel mit den Zwillingen, die sie geboren hatte, einem Knaben und Mädchen, kümmerlich lebte.
sentences.l: Thus he wandered about some years in misery and finally came into the wilderness where Rapunzel, with the twins she had borne, a boy and a girl, lived meagerly.
sentences.i: Thus he wandered about for some years in misery, and at last came into the wilderness where Rapunzel, with the twins she had borne — a boy and a girl — lived wretchedly.
sentences.n: “geboren hatte” — the past participle of gebären (‘to bear, give birth to’). The twins reveal plainly that the prince’s earlier visits had had their consequence.

sentences.t: Er vernahm eine Stimme, und sie däuchte ihn so bekannt: da gieng er darauf zu, und wie er heran kam, erkannte ihn Rapunzel und fiel ihm um den Hals und weinte.
sentences.l: He heard a voice, and it seemed to him so familiar: there he went toward it, and as he came near, Rapunzel recognized him and fell about his neck and wept.
sentences.i: He heard a voice, and it seemed to him so familiar; he went toward it, and as he came near, Rapunzel knew him, and fell upon his neck and wept.
sentences.n: “däuchte” is the archaic preterite of dünken (‘to seem’); “sie däuchte ihn bekannt” = it seemed familiar to him. “gieng ... darauf zu” (zugehen auf) and “kam ... heran” are separable. “jemandem um den Hals fallen” = to fall on someone’s neck, to embrace.

sentences.t: Zwei von ihren Thränen aber benetzten seine Augen, da wurden sie wieder klar, und er konnte damit sehen wie sonst.
sentences.l: But two of her tears moistened his eyes; then they became clear again, and he could see with them as before.
sentences.i: But two of her tears wetted his eyes, and they grew clear again, and he could see with them as before.
sentences.n: “Thränen” is the 1857 spelling of Tränen (‘tears’), with the old -th-. “wie sonst” = as before, as usual.

sentences.t: Er führte sie in sein Reich, wo er mit Freude empfangen ward, und sie lebten noch lange glücklich und vergnügt.
sentences.l: He led her into his kingdom, where he was received with joy, and they lived long yet happily and contentedly.
sentences.i: He led her into his kingdom, where he was received with joy, and they lived long and happily thereafter.
sentences.n: “ward” once more — the archaic preterite of werden, here forming the passive ‘was received.’ “glücklich und vergnügt” is a stock closing formula, ‘happy and content.’
